description: You are acting as a build engineer who must prepare a **completely-offline, reproducible Python bundle** that other teams will later consume. All wheels that may be used are already available locally; the machine has **NO Internet access**. Your goal is to: 1. create an isolated virtual-environment, 2. install three pre-supplied wheels into that environment *without* using the network, 3. generate a manifest file that precisely lists what you installed, 4. store everything in deterministic locations so automated tooling can verify it. • Your normal user is “user” and the working directory is /home/user. • A local, read-only wheel repository already exists at /home/user/package_repo. It contains **exactly** these three files (and no others): – colorama-0.4.6-py2.py3-none-any.whl – humanfriendly-10.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl – pytz-2023.3-py2.py3-none-any.whl • Python 3 and the standard “venv” module are available. 1. Create a virtual environment named buildenv directly under /home/user (i.e. its activation script must end up at /home/user/buildenv/bin/activate). 2. Install the three wheels *only* from the local directory /home/user/package_repo; the installation **must not** reach out to the Internet. 3. After the installation is complete, create a new text file /home/user/buildenv/manifest.txt that lists the packages you just installed. The manifest must follow **this exact, testable format**: • One package per line. • Lines sorted **lexicographically by package name**. • Each line must match the pattern package=<NAME>,version=<VERSION> • No extra whitespace, blank lines or BOMs. • For this particular task the file must therefore contain exactly these three lines **in the order shown**: 1. package=colorama,version=0.4.6 2. package=humanfriendly,version=10.0 3. package=pytz,version=2023.3 4. When you are done, you should have the following new items: • an activated virtual-environment at /home/user/buildenv • the manifest file at /home/user/buildenv/manifest.txt 5. The **only** files the automated checker will examine are the three wheels, the directory /home/user/buildenv and the manifest file, so make sure the manifest is perfectly formatted. • Use the standard python3 -m venv command to create the environment. • The wheels are in a local directory, therefore a typical pip invocation might involve the --no-index and --find-links flags so that pip never tries to contact PyPI. • You may verify what was installed with pip list --format=freeze or a short Python one-liner that imports the package and prints its __version__. • Be sure that the manifest lives *inside* the virtual-environment directory, at the exact path given above. • Remember the lexicographic sort order: “colorama” comes before “humanfriendly”, which comes before “pytz”. Automated tests will consider the task successful if *all* of the following hold: 1. /home/user/buildenv/manifest.txt exists. 2. The file content matches the three-line specification verbatim (byte-for-byte). 3. All three packages are importable when running /home/user/buildenv/bin/python -c "import colorama, humanfriendly, pytz". 4. No network access was required: installation must have completed using only the local wheel files under /home/user/package_repo. A typical solution requires roughly 5–10 shell commands: – create venv – activate venv – pip install (one command) – verify versions / generate manifest (1–3 commands) – optional listing / inspections – deactivate venv (optional)
truth:
<!--
     Ground-truth data used by the automated test harness.
     These values are never shown to the agent.
  -->

  <!-- 1. Pre-existing wheel repository. -->
  <preexisting_files>
    /home/user/package_repo/colorama-0.4.6-py2.py3-none-any.whl
    /home/user/package_repo/humanfriendly-10.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl
    /home/user/package_repo/pytz-2023.3-py2.py3-none-any.whl
  </preexisting_files>

  <!-- 2. Files and directories that MUST exist after the agent finishes. -->
  <expected_files_after>
    /home/user/buildenv/manifest.txt
  </expected_files_after>

  <!-- 3. Exact expected content of the manifest file. -->
  <manifest_contents>
package=colorama,version=0.4.6
package=humanfriendly,version=10.0
package=pytz,version=2023.3
  </manifest_contents>

  <!-- 4. Validation: site-packages for each package will exist under /home/user/buildenv/lib/python*/site-packages/. -->
